title: Detecting Cycles in an Undirected Graph: Fix the Backtracking

statement.markdown: Given an adjacency list representation of an undirected graph, your task is to detect whether it contains a cycle using DFS. The graph is represented as a dictionary where each key is a node and its corresponding value is a list of adjacent nodes.
statement.inputFormat: Your function should take two parameters: the graph represented as a dictionary and a set to store visited nodes.
statement.outputFormat: Your function should return True if the graph contains a cycle; otherwise, return False.
statement.notes: You should use DFS to traverse the graph and keep track of the nodes that are currently being visited. If you encounter a node that is already being visited, you know there's a cycle.

examples.explanation: The graph does not contain a cycle.

hints.text: Use a set to keep track of visited nodes and their parent nodes.

editorial.title: Cycle Detection using DFS
editorial.intuition: To detect a cycle in an undirected graph, you can use DFS to traverse the graph and keep track of the nodes being visited. If you encounter a cycle, return True; otherwise, return False.
editorial.bruteForce: Perform DFS on each unvisited node in the graph and check for cycles.
editorial.betterSolution: Use a set to store the nodes being visited and their parents.
editorial.optimalSolution: Perform DFS and check for backtracking in the recursion stack.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The algorithm visits each node at most once and backtracks when it encounters a cycle.
editorial.dryRun: Example usage of the algorithm: Given a graph with nodes A, B, C, D and edges (A, B), (B, C), (C, D), (D, A), the algorithm should return True.
commonMistakes: Not using a set to keep track of visited nodes and their parents.
Not handling the case when a node is its own parent.

functionSignature.python: def detectCycle(self, graph: dict[str, list[str]]): bool
